blocks.html:
Overview

Adult inguinal hernia pathway for primary care using urgency thresholds (emergency, urgent, routine, watchful waiting).

Key thresholds

  • Emergency: suspected strangulation or obstruction (severe pain, tender irreducible hernia, vomiting/obstructive features).
  • Urgent: all suspected groin hernias in women; irreducible or partially reducible hernia.
  • Routine: symptomatic reducible inguinal hernia (typically men) or diagnostic uncertainty.
  • Watchful waiting: minimally symptomatic/asymptomatic reducible inguinal hernia (usually men), with safety netting.

Initial assessment in primary care

  1. Confirm likely diagnosis clinically and assess reducibility.
  2. Screen immediately for emergency red flags.
  3. Document symptom burden, function impact, and patient preference.

Stepwise pathway

  1. Risk triage at first contact.
  2. Assign urgency: emergency/urgent/routine/watchful waiting.
  3. Provide conservative management where appropriate.
  4. Refer with structured information payload.

Imaging: avoid routine ultrasound for clinically obvious uncomplicated inguinal hernia; use selectively if diagnostic uncertainty.

Who to refer

  • Emergency: suspected strangulated or obstructed hernia.
  • Urgent: women with suspected groin hernia; irreducible/partially reducible hernia.
  • Routine: symptomatic reducible hernia or uncertainty.

Include in referral

  • Site/side and reducibility
  • Symptom severity and functional impact
  • Any red-flag episodes
  • Comorbidity/frailty and patient preference

Evidence confidence and notes

Confidence: Moderate. Thresholds were concordant across Right Decisions (national and board), RCS/BHS and EBI sources. NICE CKS content was not directly extractable in this run and should be checked directly during local sign-off.
